agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposed reduction of delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's compliance posture, contractual obligations, and dispute-resolution capability with severe legal and financial consequences.",
    "bounded_context": "Delivery Records / Data Lifecycle Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete audit trail of a delivery: pickup, transit events, proof of delivery, timestamps, signatures, and associated metadata. Serves as the legal source of truth for what happened." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Evidence that a consignment was delivered to the correct recipient — signatures, photos, GPS coordinates, timestamps. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and regulatory compliance." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Period", "definition": "The legally and contractually mandated minimum duration for which records must be preserved. Governed by the most restrictive applicable regulation, NOT by storage cost preferences." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The use of delivery data by business customers to satisfy their own regulatory obligations (VAT filings, customs declarations, audit trails). Deletion of upstream data destroys their compliance capability." },
      { "term": "Limitation Period", "definition": "The legal window during which claims (contractual, negligence, consumer protection) can be brought. In the UK this is 6 years; in most EU member states 3-6 years. Records must survive this window." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records must be retained for the duration required by the most restrictive applicable regulation across all operating jurisdictions (EU member states and UK).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Regulatory retention is a legal obligation, not a policy choice. Violating it exposes the company to fines, sanctions, and loss of operating licenses. No VP can override statute.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record is deleted while any applicable regulatory retention period remains unexpired."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "UK VAT records (which include delivery/shipping records for taxable supplies) must be retained for a minimum of 6 years per HMRC requirements.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "HMRC VAT Notice 700/21 mandates 6-year retention. Non-compliance results in penalties and inability to defend VAT audits.",
      "testable_assertion": "All delivery records associated with UK taxable transactions are retained for at least 6 years from the end of the relevant VAT period."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "EU member state VAT and commercial record retention requirements (typically 5-10 years depending on jurisdiction) must be satisfied for all deliveries in those jurisdictions.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Each EU member state sets its own retention period for commercial/tax records. Germany requires 10 years, France 6 years, etc. These are statutory obligations.",
      "testable_assertion": "Delivery records for each EU jurisdiction are retained for at least the minimum period required by that jurisdiction's tax/commercial law."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Delivery records must be available for the full duration of applicable civil limitation periods to support dispute resolution and litigation defense.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If records are deleted before the limitation period expires, the company cannot defend against breach of contract claims, negligence claims, or consumer complaints. This creates unquantifiable legal liability.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record is deleted while the civil limitation period for any potential claim arising from that delivery has not expired (6 years UK, 3-10 years EU depending on jurisdiction)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "B2B customers who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting must have access to that data for the duration specified in their service agreements.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a contractual obligation that could theoretically be renegotiated, but unilateral deletion would constitute breach of contract and loss of B2B customers.",
      "testable_assertion": "Delivery records accessible to B2B customers are retained for at least the period specified in each customer's service agreement or data processing agreement."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "GDPR data minimization does not override other legal retention obligations. Data may and must be retained where there is a lawful basis (legal obligation, legitimate interest for litigation defense).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR Article 17(3)(b) and (e) explicitly exempt data required for legal obligations and legal claims from the right to erasure. Deleting data to 'comply with GDPR' while violating tax law is not compliant — it is doubly non-compliant.",
      "testable_assertion": "The retention policy correctly identifies and applies lawful bases for retention that override deletion preferences."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "Chargeback and payment dispute windows (up to 540 days for some card schemes) require delivery records including proof of delivery to be available for the full dispute period.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without POD and delivery records, the company automatically loses payment disputes. At 90-day deletion, any chargeback filed after day 90 results in guaranteed financial loss.",
      "testable_assertion": "Delivery records and proof of delivery are retained for at least 540 days to cover the maximum chargeback window across all payment processors."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "A B2B customer is audited by their national tax authority 4 years after deliveries occurred and requests export of all delivery records for that period.", "expected_behavior": "All delivery records must be available and exportable. Under 90-day retention, this data would have been destroyed 3.75 years ago, causing the customer to fail their audit and exposing us to breach of contract and potential joint liability." },
    { "scenario": "A consumer files a negligence claim for a damaged high-value delivery 18 months after the fact (well within the 6-year UK limitation period).", "expected_behavior": "Full delivery records including handling events, GPS tracking, and proof of delivery must be available to mount a legal defense. Under 90-day retention, the company has zero evidence and will likely lose the claim." },
    { "scenario": "A cardholder disputes a charge 4 months after delivery, claiming non-receipt.", "expected_behavior": "Proof of delivery record is presented to the payment processor to win the dispute. Under 90-day retention, the POD has been deleted and the chargeback is automatically lost." },
    { "scenario": "HMRC or an EU tax authority conducts a VAT audit covering the past 3 years.", "expected_behavior": "All delivery records supporting VAT-liable transactions must be producible. Under 90-day retention, 97% of the audit period's records have been destroyed, resulting in penalties and potential prosecution." },
    { "scenario": "A cross-border delivery triggers a customs dispute 8 months later.", "expected_behavior": "Customs declarations, delivery routing, and proof of delivery must be available. Under 90-day retention, evidence is destroyed." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. Direct exposure includes: (1) VAT audit penalties across all EU/UK jurisdictions — potentially millions in fines and back-taxes assessed without records to defend positions. (2) 100% loss rate on any chargeback filed after day 90 — for a logistics platform this could be substantial. (3) Inability to defend civil claims up to the 6-year limitation period — unquantifiable liability. (4) B2B customer churn as they cannot use the platform for compliance. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory penalty or lost lawsuit.",
    "operational": "CRITICAL. B2B customers depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting. Destroying this data breaks their workflows, triggers SLA violations, and forces them to seek alternative providers. Internal operations (dispute handling, audit response, insurance claims) lose their evidentiary foundation.",
    "user": "HIGH. B2C consumers lose the ability to reference past deliveries for warranty claims, insurance, or disputes after 90 days. B2B users lose compliance reporting capability entirely. Trust in the platform as a reliable record-keeper is destroyed.",
    "risk": "EXTREME. This change creates simultaneous regulatory non-compliance across multiple legal frameworks (VAT, consumer protection, commercial law) in every jurisdiction the platform operates in. It also constitutes potential breach of contract with B2B customers and breach of data processing agreements. The framing as 'no business logic changes' suggests the team has not consulted legal, compliance, or finance — which is itself a process failure.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Legal & Compliance", "impact": "The company becomes non-compliant with VAT retention obligations in UK and all EU member states. Compliance team cannot respond to regulatory inquiries or audits. This is a board-level risk." },
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Customers relying on delivery data for their own compliance reporting (VAT, customs, contractual obligations) lose access to critical records. Likely triggers contract breaches and mass churn." },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "Consumers lose proof of delivery and delivery history after 90 days, undermining their ability to pursue warranty claims, insurance, or consumer protection remedies." },
      { "group": "Finance & Accounting", "impact": "Cannot produce records for VAT audits, cannot defend chargebacks beyond 90 days, cannot support financial reconciliation for prior periods." },
      { "group": "Customer Support & Disputes", "impact": "Cannot investigate or resolve any complaint about a delivery older than 90 days. Blind to historical delivery issues." },
      { "group": "Insurance & Risk", "impact": "Cannot produce evidence for insurance claims related to lost, damaged, or delayed deliveries beyond 90 days." },
      { "group": "Executive / Board", "impact": "Regulatory non-compliance is a board-level governance issue. Directors may face personal liability for knowingly destroying legally required records." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 95,
    "reasoning": "The regulatory landscape for record retention in EU/UK logistics is well-established and unambiguous. VAT retention requirements (6-10 years depending on jurisdiction), civil limitation periods (3-6 years), and chargeback windows (up to 540 days) are all matters of settled law. A 90-day retention period violates all of them by a massive margin. The only uncertainty is in the exact penalties and financial exposure, not in whether this change is permissible — it is not.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK HMRC 6-year VAT record retention requirement",
      "EU member state tax retention requirements (5-10 years)",
      "Civil limitation periods requiring records for litigation defense",
      "Chargeback window requirements exceeding 90 days",
      "B2B contractual obligations for data availability",
      "GDPR does not mandate deletion of legally required records"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact financial exposure from non-compliance (jurisdiction-specific penalty calculations)",
      "Specific B2B contract terms that may impose additional retention requirements"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform processes VAT-liable transactions in UK and EU markets",
      "B2B customer contracts include provisions for data access and compliance support",
      "The platform processes card payments subject to chargeback schemes",
      "No alternative archival system is being proposed alongside the deletion policy"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The proposal is framed as 'infrastructure optimization — no business logic changes,' but it directly violates multiple statutory retention obligations and contractual commitments. Reducing retention from 7 years to 90 days is not an infrastructure decision — it is a compliance decision that requires legal review, regulatory impact assessment, and likely board-level approval. The framing itself is dangerous because it suggests the change might bypass compliance review.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal & Compliance", "Finance", "B2B Customers", "Executive/Board", "All EU/UK end users"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B: Implement tiered retention — archive older records to cold storage (reducing cost) while maintaining legal accessibility for the full required period",
        "C: Engage legal counsel to determine the minimum defensible retention period per jurisdiction and implement jurisdiction-aware retention policies"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B (tiered storage) addresses the legitimate cost concern while maintaining compliance. Option C should be pursued in parallel to ensure the 7-year period is correctly calibrated per jurisdiction (some EU states require 10 years). Option A is the safe default until B and C are complete. Under NO circumstances should Option 90-days proceed."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "90-day retention violates UK HMRC VAT record retention requirement of 6 years", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "90-day retention violates EU member state tax/commercial record retention requirements (5-10 years)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "90-day retention destroys evidence needed for civil litigation defense within the 6-year limitation period", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "90-day retention breaches B2B customer contractual obligations for compliance data access", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" },
    { "violation": "90-day retention guarantees loss of all chargebacks filed after day 90 (window extends to 540 days)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-7" },
    { "violation": "The change is mischaracterized as 'no business logic changes' — it is a compliance-critical change requiring legal review", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" }
  ]
}
